letters.subject: The 50 words quietly weakening your writing
letters.preview: Very, just, that, basically. Cut them and watch your sentences get sharper.

affiliate.text: When I want a second pass on a draft, I run it through {{LINK}} to catch the filler I have gone blind to.

product.name: Filler-Word Killer Editor Pack
product.text: Want the cutting done for you? {{LINK}} flags all 50 filler words so you can strike them in one pass.

letters.body:
Hi there,

Filler words make English writing weak.

Writing should be concise and meaningful, especially for online audiences with shorter attention spans. Using too many filler words gives the impression of inexperience, meaning your audience may be hesitant to take you seriously.

Choosing the right word for your specific meaning and leaving out any unnecessary words will help you communicate more effectively.

Filler words are unnecessary words or phrases that add length without meaning, such as "very," "just," "that," and "basically." Unlike spoken fillers (um, uh), written filler words weaken clarity and authority.

So when you write, watch for the words that carry little meaning, don't serve a purpose, or can be removed from the sentence without altering the meaning. If a word can go and the sentence still means the same thing, let it go.

The best way to avoid filler phrases and words is to keep a filler words list on hand. That way you stop guessing and start cutting.

Read your next paragraph back and hunt for just one filler. You'll feel the sentence tighten.

Write on,
Bryan

letters.subject: The tool that catches your mistakes
letters.preview: After a decade at the comma face, here's how I pick a grammar checker.

affiliate.text: {{LINK}} is the one I reach for first when I want the mistakes caught before a reader ever sees them.

product.name: Claude Code for Writers
product.text: If you want to go past checking and actually build with AI, {{LINK}} shows you how.

letters.body:
Hi friend,

The best grammar checker is the one that catches the mistakes you keep making and fits the way you already write. That's it. That's the whole test.

After more than a decade running this site and editing thousands of posts, I've leaned on most of these tools at the comma face. And here's what I've learned: a misplaced comma or a wonky sentence structure won't sink a piece on its own. But enough of them chip away at a reader's trust before they ever reach your point.

That's where grammar checkers come in. They're no longer simple spell-checks. The better ones flag style, tone, and clarity problems a human editor would catch on a second read. They detect the spelling and punctuation slips, sure, but they also nudge you toward clearer, warmer sentences.

The leading options in 2026 are Grammarly, ProWritingAid, and Hemingway Editor. Each suits a different kind of writer and a different budget. The trick isn't finding the "best" one in the abstract. It's matching a checker to your own writing style and the errors you personally keep repeating.

Start there, and the rest sorts itself out.

Write on,
Bryan

letters.subject: The starving artist is a lie
letters.preview: Seven ways writers actually get paid now.

affiliate.text: If one of your income streams is teaching what you know, {{LINK}} is where I would host the course.

product.name: Filler-Word Killer Editor Pack
product.text: Paid work rewards clean drafts, and {{LINK}} strips the filler in one pass.

letters.body:
Hi there,

For far too long, the image of the "starving artist" has clung to writers like an unwelcome shadow. We've been told, implicitly and explicitly, that our craft is a labour of love, a passion project, and that true financial success is reserved for a lucky few.

But let me tell you, that narrative is as outdated as a typewriter.

In today's digital age, the landscape for writers has been completely redefined. The internet has democratised creativity, throwing open a myriad of doors for anyone with a command of language and a desire to share their voice. This isn't just about making ends meet. It's about building a robust, diversified financial life that lets you thrive, not just survive.

Income streams for writers include freelance writing, self-publishing, content marketing, online courses, affiliate marketing, ghostwriting, and coaching.

You don't need all seven. But you do need more than one. Authors like Mark Dawson and Joanna Penn built six-figure careers by diversifying beyond a single source. The lesson isn't that they got lucky. It's that they stopped betting everything on one door.

Pick one stream you haven't tried. Open that door this week.

Write on,
Bryan

letters.subject: The line that fixed my nervous character
letters.preview: Not "she was nervous." Something you can feel instead.

affiliate.text: When I go back through a draft hunting for flat, tell-y sentences, I run it through {{LINK}} first. Its sensory and pacing checks flag the lines begging to be shown.

product.name: Filler-Word Killer Editor Pack
product.text: Once the telling is fixed, the filler is next. {{LINK}} flags all 50 filler words in one pass.

letters.body:
Hi friend,

Here is the most misunderstood piece of writing advice going: show, don't tell.

It does not mean never use the verb "to be." It does not mean avoid all adjectives, or describe everything in visual detail. It means this: when you have the chance to make a reader feel something rather than just understand it, prefer the former.

Instead of "she was nervous," you write "she pressed her thumbnail into the meat of her palm." The difference is between information and experience. Telling gives information. Showing creates experience. Both are necessary. The real question is which one serves the scene.

My favourite example is Carver's "Cathedral." He never tells us the narrator is jealous and hostile. He shows it through behaviour: the clipped, dismissive descriptions of the blind man, the refusal to engage, the drinking. The emotion is in the action, not the statement.

So it is a useful default, not an absolute rule. Summary and telling have their place too. But next time you catch yourself writing "he was angry," stop. Ask what he does with his hands instead.

Write on,
Bryan

letters.subject: Grammarly is not the right fit for everyone
letters.preview: The right tool depends on what you're actually writing.

affiliate.text: If you want to see whether Grammarly earns its spot in your stack, you can try {{LINK}}.

product.name: Claude Code for Writers
product.text: When you are ready to build with AI instead of just checking with it, {{LINK}} is the guide.

letters.body:
Hi there,

Grammarly dominates the grammar checker space. But after testing every tool on my alternatives list, I keep coming back to one thing: it is not the right fit for everyone.

The best alternative depends entirely on your use case. LanguageTool if you want open-source grammar checking. QuillBot for paraphrasing. Hemingway App for readability and style. ProWritingAid for long-form manuscript editing. ChatGPT for AI-assisted drafting. Wordtune for sentence rewriting. Each one serves a different writing need, and none of them is trying to be everything at once.

So whether you need a free option, a tool built for academic writing, an AI that generates content, a dedicated paraphraser, or a focused readability editor, there is a better fit for your specific situation.

It is also worth knowing what Grammarly has become. In 2024 the company renamed its paid Premium plan to Grammarly Pro, so the lineup is now Free, Pro, and a custom-priced Enterprise plan. Still a strong product. Just no longer the only sensible answer.

Pick the tool that matches the writing in front of you, not the one with the biggest name.

Write on,
Bryan

letters.subject: The snowball that gets your book written
letters.preview: A reader asked how I balance daily content with the books. Here's the honest answer.

affiliate.text: When I want to learn craft from writers at the top of the game, I watch {{LINK}}.

product.name: Filler-Word Killer Editor Pack
product.text: When you do sit down to edit, {{LINK}} makes the cutting quick so momentum survives.

letters.body:
Hi friend,

A reader asked me a question on social media a few days ago: "How do you find the time for daily articles and newsletter content AND the time to actually write your books?"

It's a common struggle. You want to produce consistent content while also working on longer, more substantial projects. And when I try to do both at once, I feel tense and stressed, like bending a stick until it snaps.

So I don't. I pick one project to work on first thing. For a few years, that project was a book. Like a snowball rolling downhill, starting with 300 words creates momentum.

When I had a full-time job as a copywriter for a fintech, I got up around five or six to work on my books. I gave up gaming on my Xbox late at night. I still missed it, but I enjoyed writing more than levelling up in Call of Duty.

Then I quit my job to work full-time at my business. I canned the 5am starts because I was sick of looking at the bags under my eyes. Now I start around nine, when the kids are at school and the house is quiet.

The season changes. The one-project rule doesn't.

Write on,
Bryan

letters.subject: The hardest form to master (and the best place to learn)
letters.preview: Why short stories teach you more than a novel ever will.

affiliate.text: When your draft is done, the editing is where a short story is won or lost. I run mine through {{LINK}} to catch the over-explanation and slow spots.

product.name: Filler-Word Killer Editor Pack
product.text: The other thief of a tight story is filler. {{LINK}} flags all 50 in one pass.

letters.body:
Hi,

Short stories are the hardest form to master and the best place to learn to write. They give you immediate feedback on every decision, character, voice, structure, scene, without the long commitment of a novel.

Here is the thing most writers miss: a short story is not a small novel. It works differently. It has to do its work in a single sustained movement, usually focusing on one character, one situation, and one significant change.

The form rewards compression, implication, precision of detail, and endings that resonate rather than resolve. It punishes over-explanation, multiple plot threads, slow openings, and anything that does not serve the central situation.

That word, situation, is where I want you to start. Every short story needs a central situation: a specific circumstance that creates pressure on a character. This is not the same as a plot. A plot is what happens. A central situation is the condition that makes things happen.

A woman learns her estranged father is dying. Two strangers are trapped in a lift. A soldier returns home to find everything familiar has become strange.

Each of those creates immediate pressure. Find yours, and the story starts writing itself.

Write on,
Bryan

letters.subject: The honest truth about AI writing tools
letters.preview: Most of them run on the same engine anyway.

affiliate.text: If you want a checker that catches the slips before a reader does, you can try {{LINK}}.

product.name: Claude Code for Writers
product.text: If you want to go past templates and actually build with AI, {{LINK}} shows you how.

letters.body:
Hi there,

Every week someone asks me which AI writing tool is best. ChatGPT? Jasper? Copy.ai? The list feels endless, and it keeps growing. AI is expected to see an annual growth rate of 37.3% from 2023 to 2030, so the flood of new tools is not slowing down.

Here is the part nobody selling you a subscription wants to say out loud: most of these applications use the same large language models to power content creation. So do not worry too much about the quality of the work. They are quite similar under the hood.

That changes the question you should be asking. Instead of hunting for the "best" one, look at the different features, benefits, and templates, and opt for the software that is most relevant to your needs.

After testing a stack of them, I found ChatGPT, Jasper, Copy.ai, and GrammarlyGO are your best bets. They are affordable, produce quality outputs, and keep releasing features that streamline your writing process.

Pick the one that fits how you actually work, then stop shopping and start writing.

Write on,
Bryan

letters.subject: The best apps for taking notes while you read
letters.preview: Evernote, Notion, Apple Notes and more, plus the habit that beats all of them.

affiliate.text: If you want to learn the craft from writers who have mastered it, {{LINK}} is where I would start.

product.name: The Zettelkasten for Creators Kit
product.text: Capturing notes is step one. Turning them into finished writing is the part most people miss, and {{LINK}} shows the system I use.

letters.body:
Hi friend,

Whether you're a student, researcher, or writer, taking notes while you read is an important skill.

Many of us grew up taking notes the old-school way, with a paper and pad. But technology has made note-taking simpler, more efficient, and more comfortable. Our reading habits are now closely linked to our phones and tablets, and several good note-taking apps have arrived in recent years.

The best apps for taking notes while reading include Evernote, Notion, Apple Notes, Google Keep, and Kindle's built-in highlight tool. Each one helps you capture ideas digitally, across every device you own.

The big draw is convenience. A good app gives you features a pen and pad never could, and you can reach your notes from most devices, including your smartphone or tablet.

So pick the one that suits how you already read. The tool matters less than the habit of catching a good idea before it slips away.

Write on,
Bryan
